coredata.dc:title: Covalently attached strategy to modulate surface of carbon quantum dots: Towards effectively multifunctional lubricant additives in polar and apolar base fluids
coredata.dc:creator: Ye, Mengting
Cai, Tao
Zhao, Lina
Liu, Dan
Liu, Shenggao
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots with multifunctional properties (CQDs-N) as lubricant additive in polar base fluid of bio-based castor oil (CO) were first studied. Followed, a liquid-like CQDs-N with oleylamine capped on the surface (O-CQDs-N) was achieved through chemical grafting route, which could dissolve in apolar base fluid of polyalphaolefin (PAO). Either CQDs-N or O-CQDs-N exhibited excellent dispersion stabilities in CO and PAO, which not only endowed them with enhanced anti-wear performance, but also anti-oxidant performance. The carbon quantum dots-based nanoparticles deposited on the worn surface unevenly and formed a thin protective film containing iron oxides, inorganic carbon and nitrogen. Furthermore, the deposited carbon was proved to produce more defects and disordered structure when test load increased, which enhanced the lubricating performance and loading capacity of base fluids.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots-based nanoadditives
Polar and apolar base fluids
Anti-oxidation
Anti-wear

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Structure characterization and analysis of CQDs-N and O-CQDs-N
Tribological behavior of CQDs-N and O-CQDs-N as lubricant additives
Surface analysis of the worn surfaces

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of (a1) O-CQDs-N and (b1) CQDs-N, and their size distribution (a2, b2).
PL emission spectra at different excitation wavelengths of (a) O-CQDs-N and (b) CQDs-N, the insert images are O-CQDs-N and CQDs-N under sunlight (left) and irradiated by 365 nm light (right).
FT-IR spectra of CQDs-N, O-CQDs-N and OA.
XPS wide spectra, fine XPS structure spectra of C 1s and O 1s of O-CQDs-N (a, c, e) and CQDs-N (b, d, f).
TGA curves of O-CQDs-N and CQDs-N.
Digital images and dispersion stability of (a) PAO with 2 wt% O-CQDs-N and (b) CO with 0.2 wt% CQDs-N as a function of time. Time for each image is noted on the respective dispersion sample bottle.
Antioxidant responses of (a) CQDs-N in CO and (b) O-CQDs-N in PAO with different concentration evaluated by isothermal PDSC at 170 °C.
(a) Friction coefficient curves and (b) wear scar diameters lubricated by CO with CQDs-N at different concentrations (load = 392 N; rotate speed = 1200 rpm; duration = 60 min; temperature = 75 °C).

3D images and the corresponding height profiles (red lines) of the wear scar lubricated by (a) CO and (b) 0.2 wt% CQDs-N in CO, respectively. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a) Friction coefficient curves and (b) wear scar diameters lubricated by PAO with O-CQDs-N at different concentrations (load = 392 N; rotate speed = 1200 rpm; duration = 60 min; temperature = 75 °C).

3D images and the corresponding height profiles (red lines) of the wear scar lubricated by (a) PAO and (b) 0.5 wt% O-CQDs-N in PAO, respectively. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Mean wear scar diameters lubricated by PAO and PAO with 0.5 wt% O-CQDs-N at different load (rotate speed = 1200 rpm; duration = 60 min; temperature = 75 °C).

Friction coefficient curves lubricated by PAO and PAO with 0.5 wt% O-CQDs-N at (a) 490 N and (b) 588 N, respectively, the insert images are wear scars lubricated by PAO at corresponding load (rotate speed = 1200 rpm; duration = 60 min; temperature = 75 °C).
(a1, b1, c1, d1) SEM images and (a2, b2, c2, d2) carbon elemental mappings of worn surfaces lubricated by PAO at 392 N and PAO with 0.5 wt% O-CQDs-N at 196 N, 392 N and 588 N.

(a1, b1, c1) images and (a2, b2, c2) Raman mappings of worn surfaces lubricated by PAO with 0.5 wt% O-CQDs-N at 196 N, 392 N and 588 N. The change of color of a2, b2, c2 represented the size of area of D- and G-bands. a3, b3, c3 are Raman spectra corresponding to the specified areas in the a2, b2, c2. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Curve-fitted XPS spectra of (a) Fe2p, (b) C1s, (c) N1s, (d) O1s of the worn surface lubricated by PAO with 0.5 wt% O-CQDs-N at 392 N.
Structure features of the carbon quantum dots studied in this work.
The proposed tribological mechanism of O-CQDs-N.

head.ce:title: Covalently attached strategy to modulate surface of carbon quantum dots: Towards effectively multifunctional lubricant additives in polar and apolar base fluids

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Ningbo Institute of Materials Technology and Engineering, Chinese Academy of Sciences, No. 1219, Zhongguan West Road, Ningbo City, Zhejiang Province, 315201, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Ningbo Institute of Materials Technology and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing, 100049, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots with multifunctional properties (CQDs-N) as lubricant additive in polar base fluid of bio-based castor oil (CO) were first studied. Followed, a liquid-like CQDs-N with oleylamine capped on the surface (O-CQDs-N) was achieved through chemical grafting route, which could dissolve in apolar base fluid of polyalphaolefin (PAO). Either CQDs-N or O-CQDs-N exhibited excellent dispersion stabilities in CO and PAO, which not only endowed them with enhanced anti-wear performance, but also anti-oxidant performance. The carbon quantum dots-based nanoparticles deposited on the worn surface unevenly and formed a thin protective film containing iron oxides, inorganic carbon and nitrogen. Furthermore, the deposited carbon was proved to produce more defects and disordered structure when test load increased, which enhanced the lubricating performance and loading capacity of base fluids.

ce:section.ce:para: To extend the service life of the equipment and reduce economic losses, researchers have to explore new and high performance lubricants or lubricant additives to meet the growing demand [1,2]. Various types of carbon-based nanoparticles (CNPs) have been promising candidates for lubricant additives due to their excellent tribological properties and little pollution to the environment, such as graphene, carbon nanotubes and fullerene as well as their derivatives [3–7]. It is widely accepted that the well-dispersed CNPs in base fluids can behave their full potential and distinguished performance. However, the dispersion stability of CNPs in base fluids is still waiting to be solved.

As the new kind of CNPs, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and their derivatives with extremely small size and unique properties have attracted much attention to study their lubricant properties. Thanks to its oxygen-containing groups and facile preparing methods, CQDs has been a potential lubricant additive with anti-wear and friction-reducing properties [8]. In fact, owing to its own strong polarity of CQDs, a limited number of CQDs-based nanoparticles have been studied for strengthening the lubricating properties of polar polyethylene glycol (PEG). For instance, the previous researches reported on uses of CQDs [9], nitrogen-doped or nitrogen/boron-codoped carbon dots [10,11], carbon dots-ionic liquid hybrid nanomaterial as lubricant additives [12,13], and each of them exhibited excellent anti-wear and friction-reducing performances in PEG. The dispersion stability of CQDs in polar PEG base fluids has been improved significantly owing to the interactions of the oxygen-containing groups in CQDs and PEG, thereby facilitating the long-term stability.

Vegetable oil, such as bio-based castor oil (CO), has been considered as the potential lubricant in replacement of mineral oils in recent years [14]. Thanks to polar groups of the fatty acid, oily molecules of the castor oil are easily absorbed on contact surface, thereby forming a tribofilm. However, neat castor oil cannot provide effective lubricity and there are still lack suitable additives to improve its lubricity. Therefore, it is plausible to explore the CQDs as the nanoadditive for castor oil. On the other side, the polar feature of CQDs brings the compatibility issue with apolar hydrocarbon oils, such as polyalphaolefin (PAO), which limits the potential application of CQDs nanoparticles.

The main aims of this study include two aspects. The one is to explore the multi-functional CQDs (CQDs-N) as the nanoadditive in bio-based polar castor oil (CO), which has been prepared according to our previous work [15]. CQDs-N has hydroxyl, carboxyl and some diphenylamine groups on the surface, which not only enhance the tribological properties, but also bring anti-oxidant performance. On the other side, many researchers have modified the carbon nanomaterials and other nanoparticles with long chain hydrocarbons to improve the dispersion stability in oil [16–21]. Therefore, the second aim of this study is to prepare apolar CQDs-N (O-CQDs-N) through covalently attached strategy. Unlike large quantities of hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on the surface of polar CQDs, the diphenylamine groups in CQDs-N bring steric hindrance effect and hydrophobicity, which makes the covalently attached groups relatively weak bond to the surface of CQDs-N. So the solvent method is used to improve the uniformity of reaction and the dispersion stability [22]. Followed, the anti-oxidation and anti-wear properties of the CQDs-N in castor oil (CO) and O-CQDs-N in PAO were investigated. It is worth mentioning that CQDs has never applied to CO as the multifunctional lubricant additive, and the multifunctional O-CQDs-N in PAO has not reported yet. The structure and key physicochemical performances of O-CQDs-N were characterized with HR-TEM, FT-IR, TGA, PL and XPS. The dispersion stabilities of CQDs-N in CO and O-CQDs-N in PAO were studied by UV–vis, which both exhibited long-term stability. Then their anti-oxidation and anti-wear properties were evaluated by PDSC and four-ball tester. The lubrication mechanism was examined by SEM-EDS, Raman spectroscopy and XPS.

Citric acid monohydrate (CA, 99.5%), Oleyl amine (OA, AR grade) and 4-Aminodiphenylamine (ADPA, 98%) were purchased from Aladdin Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (China) and used without further purification. Commercial available polyalphaolefin (PAO) and castor oil (CO) were purchased from Mobil and Aladdin Chemical Reagent, respectively, and were used as the base fluids. The kinematic viscosity of PAO at 40 °C and 100 °C is 30.80 and 5.83 mm2/s, respectively. All the organic solvents were purchased from Shanghai Chemical Reagent.

The structures of CQDs-N and O-CQDs-N were shown in Scheme 1 . CQDs-N, which was prepared as reported previously [15], and OA were added to a 250 ml round bottomed flask at a mass ratio of 1:5.5 dissolved in the mixture solvent and stirred for 6 h at 150 °C. Water were generated and removed. Finally O-CQDs-N was obtained by removing the solvent through vacuum distillation, extracting, removing solvent and vacuum drying, respectively.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained by a JEOL2100 system. Fluorescence spectra were acquired on the fluorescence spectrometer (FL3-111, HORIBA). The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrophotometer (NICOLET 6700, Thermo) was used to measure the structure of prepared products. X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS) was performed on the Axis Ultra DLD spectrometer to measure the chemical composition of prepared products and information on the worn surface. Thermo-gravimetric analysis (TGA) was measured by a Diamond TG instrument heated from 50 to 600 °C at a rate of 10 °C/min in nitrogen flow. Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectra were recorded by a Lambda 950 spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer). 3D wear scar images were acquired by a Zeiss LSM700 optical microscope. Zeiss EVO18 scanning electron microscope (SEM) with energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) was used to acquire morphologies and elemental mappings of worn surfaces. Raman spectra were collected by a Renishaw Invia Reflex spectrometer.

The tribological properties of oil samples were conducted on a four-ball tester (Tianji, Xiamen, China) using AISI52100 steel balls (12.7 mm diameter) under boundary lubrication (load, 196/392/490/588 N; rotating rate, 1200 rpm; temperature, 75 °C; duration, 60 min).

The pressure differential scanning calorimetry (PDSC) test of oil samples was measured by a computerized NETZSCH DSC204 HP instrument (Bavarian, Germany). Oxidation induction time (OIT) was the time from the start of adding oxygen to the appearance of the exothermic peak. The oil samples were tested from 50 °C to 170 °C at a heating rate of 50 °C/min and then held in an isothermal mode for 2 min. The oxygen was added at a rate of 100 mL/min until the maximal pressure up to 3.5 MPa.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structure characterization and analysis of CQDs-N and O-CQDs-N
ce:section.ce:para: For the practical purpose, it is significant and prospective to adjust the polarity of CQDs-N to apply to engine oil in big demand. On the other hand, green and environmental protection of CQDs-N without S, P and Cl elements meets the requirement of low sulfated ash, phosphorous and sulfur (SAPS) and halogen-free high performance lubricant additives. As for the method of adjustment of its polarity, oleylamine is chosen to covalent graft on the surface of CQDs-N based on our previous work, which can stabilize the surface of particles for its strong coordinating effect.

TEM was used to characterize the morphology of O-CQDs-N. O-CQDs-N (Fig. 1 a) showed pseudo-spherical shape with a mean diameter of 4 nm, which is slightly larger than that of CQDs-N (Fig. 1b). Fig. 2 displayed the PL emission spectra of O-CQDs-N and CQDs-N. O-CQDs-N showed the maximum excitation (λex) and emission (λem) wavelengths at 380 nm and 430 nm, respectively. However, the maximum λex and λem of CQDs-N were 420 nm and 450 nm, respectively. In conclusion, the maximum λex and λem of O-CQDs-N both exhibited blue-shift after the adjustment of polarity.

In order to characterize the structure of O-CQDs-N, the FT-IR spectra were obtained and shown in the Fig. 3 . The spectrum of O-CQDs-N exhibited some characteristic peaks attributed to OA, such as stretching vibration peaks of C-H in the range of 3005–2854 cm−1 and the rocking vibration peak of -(CH2)n- (n ≥ 4) at 722 cm−1. Compared to the spectrum of CQDs-N, the stretching vibration peak of O-H decreased sharply and the stretching vibration peak of C=O in amido appeared in the spectrum of O-CQDs-N, which indicated the amidation reaction between carboxyl and amino group. The above analyses can demonstrated the existence of long chains of alkyl in the O-CQDs-N.

XPS spectra were used to further analyze the chemical compositions of O-CQDs-N and CQDs-N. The wide spectra (Fig. 4 a and b) showed that contents of carbon and nitrogen of O-CQDs-N both obviously increased while the content of oxygen significantly decreased compared with CQDs-N. It can be inferred that the reaction between –COOH on the surface of CQDs-N and –NH2 of OA caused changes in the elemental contents. Moreover, the contents of C-C and C-O/C-N in the fine C 1s spectrum (Fig. 4c and d) of O-CQDs-N increased, indicating long chains of alkyl were grafted on the carbon cores after modified with OA. And the reduction of the content of C-O in fine O 1s spectrum (Fig. 4e and f) of O-CQDs-N further explained the decrease of –COOH. The results of XPS consistent with FT-IR confirmed OA successfully grafted on the carbon cores of O-CQDs-N.

TGA was used to measure the thermal stability and relative mass fraction of chemical grafted O-CQDs-N (Fig. 5 ). The initial weight loss of O-CQDs-N began at ∼200 °C higher than that of CQDs-N at ∼150 °C, indicating the enhanced thermal stability of O-CQDs-N compared to CQDs-N. The thermal residue fraction of O-CQDs-N and CQDs-N was found to be 0.92 wt% and 14.99 wt%, respectively. Because OA can be totally decomposed before 600 °C, the long chains of alkyl grafted on the O-CQDs-N also decomposed in the same temperature range which decreased the thermal residue fraction of O-CQDs-N. Moreover, the relative mass fraction of chemical grafted O-CQDs-N was 93.9 wt% according to the results.

The dispersion stability of PAO with 2 wt% O-CQDs-N was evaluated by the UV intensity as a function of time. As the Fig. 6 a shown, the relative absorbance of the dispersion decreased slowly after a long period of standing and little sedimentation was observed for the dispersion with O-CQDs-N after 90 days. This result indicated that the dispersion of PAO with O-CQDs-N exhibited well dispersion stability. The dispersion stability derived from the long hydrocarbon segments on the surface of O-CQDs-N, which can easily stretch into PAO base oil and produce steric hindrance effect to prevent the coagulating of nanoparticles [23]. Similarly, the dispersion of CO with 0.2 wt% CQDs-N was still stable and homogeneous after 150 day's standing (Fig. 6b) because of the interactions of the oxygen-containing groups in CQDs-N and CO.

The oxidative stabilities of CQDs-N in CO and O-CQDs-N in PAO with different concentration were evaluated, respectively. Based on previous works [24–26], isothermal PDSC was used to measure the oxidative stability because of the quickness and accuracy.

As Fig. 7 shown, the OIT of CO and PAO without any additive were 4.5 and 3.4 min, respectively. Additions of CQDs-N to CO and O-CQDs-N to PAO both could prolong the time of appearing the exothermic peak. More specifically, after adding 0.1 wt%, 0.2 wt%, 0.3 wt% and 0.4 wt% CQDs-N to CO, the OIT achieved 9.7, 11.2, 13.6 and 14.7 min, respectively. And the OIT of PAO with 0.1 wt%, 0.25 wt%, 0.5 wt%, 1 wt% and 2 wt% O-CQDs-N were 14.8, 21.9, 28.2, 36.9 and 57.7 min, respectively.

The antioxidant properties of CQDs-N in CO and O-CQDs-N in PAO are derived from the structure of diphenylamine, which acts as the radical scavenger. Alkoxy or alkyl peroxy radical groups, which produced during the chain oxidation process of the base oil, can capture the active hydrogen atom on the amine group of diphenylamine, thereby causing the interruption of the chain reaction. Thus, CQDs-N and O-CQDs-N can delay the oxidation process of CO and PAO, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Tribological behavior of CQDs-N and O-CQDs-N as lubricant additives
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 8 showed the friction results for CQDs-N at different concentration in CO. It was observed that the friction coefficient curve of CO first appeared a broad spike (>0.08) within ∼600 s and then gradually dropped to stable after ∼1200 s (∼0.04). It indicated that the “running-in” duration was long and a low-friction surface film formed, because CO had a special structure with long hydrocarbon chains and polar groups, which could act as an organic friction modifier [27,28]. However, the addition of CQDs-N to CO remained curves stable (∼0.06) nearly without fluctuation during the whole friction process, except the gradual rise appearing on the curve of 0.1 wt% CQDs-N after ∼2400 s. The results showed that the addition of CQDs-N had no friction-reducing property for CO. The reason may be that strong polar CQDs-N leaded competing adsorption with the base oil on the friction surface, which destroyed the self-assemble of CO molecules, thus an efficient low-friction surface film cannot build up [29]. The wear results (Fig. 8b) showed that CQDs-N in CO at extremely low concentration of 0.1 wt% obviously reduced the wear scar diameters (WSD) compared with pure CO. With the increase of concentration of CQDs-N, the WSD was further reduced and reached the minimum value at 0.335 mm (reduced by 44.7%) at the optimum concentration of 0.2 wt%. In order to observe the wear scars more intuitively, 3D images were shown in Fig. 9 . The worn surface lubricated by CO was rougher with burrs and deeper than that lubricated by CO with 0.2 wt% CQDs-N, which proved CQDs-N as the additive to CO had excellent anti-wear property.

The tribological properties of 0.5 wt% O-CQDs-N in PAO at different load were further investigated as shown in Fig. 12 . WSD of PAO at 196 N, 294 N and 392 N were 0.656 mm, 0.803 mm and 0.869 mm, respectively, while WSD of PAO with 0.5 wt% O-CQDs-N were 0.578 mm (reduced by 11.9%), 0.602 mm (reduced by 25.0%) and 0.869 mm (reduced by 28.5%), respectively. It was found that PAO did not work at 490 N and 588 N though the four balls test. As Fig. 13 shown, the value of the coefficient of friction was so high (>0.20) that forced the test stopped within 30 s, and the friction surface was seriously worn (the wear scar diameter was 0.752 and 0.831 mm at 490 N and 588 N, respectively). However, tribological tests of PAO with 0.5 wt% O-CQDs-N at 490 N and 588 N could successfully finished and corresponding WSD were 0.729 mm and 0.818 mm, respectively. It was evident that there was an effectively improvement of anti-wear property and loading capacity for PAO when O-CQDs-N applied as the lubricant additive.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface analysis of the worn surfaces
ce:section.ce:para: In order to understand the lubricant mechanism of O-CQDs-N, SEM-EDS and Micro-Raman spectroscopy were used to characterize the worn surface. In the Fig. 14 , SEM-EDS images showed that a large number of carbon elements distributed along the trace of the friction surface. Furthermore, the deposited carbon in micrometer scale was found on the worn surface when the tribological testing load was up to 588 N (Fig. 14d). Combined with the results that carbon elements were distributed uniformly on the worn surface lubricated without O-CQDs-N (Fig. 14 a2) and the Atomic % of the worn surface lubricated with O-CQDs-N under different load (Table S1), it can infer that the higher load applied the more carbon deposited on the friction surface. Micro-Raman spectroscopy was used to further analyze the worn surface shown in Fig. 15 . Raman spectra of the worn surface lubricated by PAO only detected a characteristic peak of iron oxides at ∼700 cm−1, which demonstrated the steel surface was oxidized during friction (figure S2). However, it found peaks at ∼1350 and ∼1580 cm−1 attributed to D-band and G-band of carbon characteristic bands, respectively, which suggested the existence of deposited carbon developed on worn surface after adding the O-CQDs-N to PAO (Fig. 15). Furthermore, the distribution of deposited carbon was detected by Raman mapping in which the red area represented the area with most carbon deposition. The irregular distribution of different color suggested that deposited carbon distributed unevenly on the worn surface, which could be attributed to the uniform load distribution and roughness of friction interfaces [31]. In order to investigate the change of the structure of deposited carbon, the intensity ratio of D-band and G-band (ID/IG) was calculated. Raman spectra showed that with the increase of load the value of ID/IG was increased (Fig. 15 a3, b3, c3). This result suggested that disordered carbon deposited on the friction surface with defect structure [31,32]. Furthermore, the disordered degree and the defect level of deposited carbon transformed to high when the test load increased, indicating friction induced the structure of carbon nanoparticles additives change [33–35]. Interestingly, when the test load was low to 196 N, it found the existence of fluorescent carbon of which the fluorescence was so intense that covered the characteristics (figure S3) [36]. It had been confirmed that the fluorescent carbon was carbon dots deposited on the worn surface. However, Raman spectra did not detected fluorescent carbon when the test load further increased. All the results indicated that O-CQDs-N could absorb on friction surface and form a uneven carbon protective film on worn surface during friction process, which could reduce the direct contact of friction pairs. Moreover, with the load increased, friction could induce deposited carbon to produce more defects and more disordered structure.

XPS analysis was used to further detect chemical composition on the worn surface lubricated by PAO with 0.5 wt% O-CQDs-N at 392 N. In Fig. 16 , XPS observed the peaks of Fe2p, C1s, N1s and O1s, which were attributed to the deposition of O-CQDs-N on the worn surface. In Fig. 16a, fine XPS spectrum of Fe2p was fitted to four peaks at 706.6, 710.5, 719.2 and 724.0 eV [37], respectively. Combined with the peak at 529.7 eV in the spectrum of O1s attributed to iron oxides, peaks of Fe2p were corresponded to Fe2p3/2 for Fe and Fe2O3, Fe2p1/2 for Fe and Fe2O3, respectively. In the spectrum of C1s, absorption peak at 284.8 eV belonged to C=C/C-C, 285.8 and 288.6 eV belonged to C-O/C-N and C=O, respectively. As for N1s t spectrum, the peak at 400.0 eV of was assigned to organic amine [38]. The above analysis indicated that O-CQDs-N absorbed and deposited on the worn surface to form a protective film thus preventing the direct contact of friction pairs to exhibit excellent anti-wear property.

According to above analyses of the worn surface, the reasonable lubricating mechanism of O-CQDs-N was thereby proposed and shown in Scheme 2 . At first, the metal surface emits low-energy electrons with the friction force and generates positive charges on the tiny convex volume [39]. O-CQDs-N can absorb onto the positive surface by physical absorption and electrostatic attraction and act as bearing balls with rolling effect to reduce the friction at initial stage. As test time goes on, the traction and compression created by contact pressure destroy the long hydrocarbon segments of O-CQDs-N and even O-CQDs-N. When the testing load increases, which cause higher temperature and pressure, more O-CQDs-N deposit on the friction surface even in micrometer scale and produce more defects and disordered structure. Thus, a thin protective film that contains iron oxides, inorganic carbon and nitrogen element is formed on the worn surface, which avoids direct contact of the friction pairs and bears the load from steel balls. As a result, O-CQDs-N as additives in PAO can effectively improve anti-wear property and loading capacity.

In summary, this work successfully synthesized oleylamine-modified CQDs-N (O-CQDs-N) and illustrated the potential of CQDs-N and O-CQDs-N as candidate lubricant additives to CO and PAO base oils with anti-wear and anti-oxidation performances. The CQDs-N and O-CQDs-N were characterized by TEM, Fluorescence spectrometer, FT-IR, XPS, TGA and UV–vis spectrometry. Results showed the size of O-CQDs-N was slightly larger than CQDs-N. The dispersions of CQDs-N in CO and O-CQDs-N in PAO both exhibited long-term stability, which also enhanced anti-oxidation properties of CO and PAO, respectively. The tribological test proved the very small amount of addition (0.2 wt%) of CQDs-N to CO reduced WSD by 44.7%, exhibiting excellent anti-wear property. O-CQDs-N as lubricant additive enhanced the anti-wear property of PAO at different concentrations. Importantly, it was found that the addition of O-CQDs-N effectively improved the loading capacity of PAO. SEM, Raman and XPS analysis demonstrated O-CQDs-N deposited on the worn surface unevenly to form a thin protective film containing iron oxides, inorganic carbon and nitrogen element, which avoided direct contact of the friction pairs and bore the load from steel balls. Furthermore, the carbon deposited on the friction surface was proved to produce more defects and disordered structure when test load increased, which enhanced lubricating performance and loading capacity of PAO.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grand No. 21404114 and 21507139), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (Grand No. 2014M561797), and the Natural Science Foundation of Ningbo (Grant No. 2015A610051 and 2016A610265).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Supporting Information Supporting Information

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.triboint.2019.03.045.

Graphene-based engine oil nanofluids for tribological applications
